pageSubtitle: Contract activity by day — risk and volume heatmap
headline: The calendar of suspicious spending
headlineSubtitle: Contract activity and risk level by day. Year-end and election-year patterns reveal systematic behavior.
lede: Each cell represents one day. Colors indicate the average risk level of contracts awarded that day: deep red marks days where most contracts show risk factors. December patterns are especially revealing — year-end "budget dumps" concentrate contracts that under normal circumstances would have gone through competitive bidding.

electionBanner.title: Federal election year
electionBanner.body: Contracts in the pre-election period may show atypical patterns of direct award. Compare the months preceding the election with non-election years.

error.load: Calendar data could not be loaded. COMPRANET may be temporarily unavailable — showing cached data if available.
error.noData: No data available for {{year}}. COMPRANET records for this year have not yet been processed or the year is outside the coverage range (2015–2024).

patterns.title: Detected patterns — {{year}}
patterns.decemberSpikeDetail: {{ratio}}x the monthly average — budget close detected
patterns.electionDetail: Federal elections in {{year}} — contracting typically accelerates in the months preceding the election as outgoing governments rush spending

decemberSection.title: RUBLI · December Under the Microscope
decemberSection.noData: December data not available for {{year}}. The selected year may have partial data.
decemberSection.subtitle: December's budget close concentrates accelerated contracting. In {{year}}, December accumulated {{dec}} contracts against a monthly average of {{avg}}.

finding.label: FINDING
finding.body: 24 consecutive years of spending spikes in December. The budget close is structural, not accidental. Every administration, regardless of party, exhausts remaining budget in December — creating a predictable window of accelerated, lower-scrutiny contracting that concentrates a disproportionate share of high-risk awards.
finding.oecd: OECD: Mexico's direct award rate exceeds 80% — more than 3x the recommended limit of 25%.

source: Source: COMPRANET · 3.06M contracts (2002–2025) · Risk model v0.8.5 (AUC=0.785)
